title: Sleeping Gods - Questions and Answers and Literature Discussion (NO SPOILERS)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DIv0FHIZiko

Daniel (@dungeondive) follows his first full Sleeping Gods campaign with a spoiler-free Q&A and a generous nautical reading list. The headline answer: this is the first Ryan Laukat / Red Raven game he has truly loved. He's long admired the art and world-building of City of Iron, Empires of the Void 2, Architects, Above and Below and Near and Far, but never gelled with their gameplay — the Euro mechanisms always felt bolted onto the story. Sleeping Gods, he argues, is the first to fuse the two into a cohesive whole you never want to ignore.

For comparisons he reaches past board games: take the best of Tales of the Arabian Nights, the Fabled Lands gamebooks and the app 80 Days, and you get something close. It's unlike Forgotten Waters or Jerry Hawthorne's page-contained adventure-book games — this is an open atlas — and unlike the puzzle-cold Seventh Continent, because here he felt genuine emotional attachment to the crew. He's emphatic it's a true solo experience, hamstrung by the alpha-player problem in groups, and confirms a retail release is imminent.

The reading list is a love letter to seafaring fantasy: Le Guin's Earthsea, Hugh Cook's The Walrus and the Warwolf, Moorcock's Elric, Merritt's The Ship of Ishtar, Tim Powers' On Stranger Tides and more.

----

Which nautical fantasy — book, film, or game — best captures the romance of the sea for you? Any on Daniel's list you'd push to the top?
